machine: granite-rapids (c8i.2xlarge, Xeon 6975P-C, 8 vCPU, 15GB RAM; L1d 48KB/core, L2 2MB/core, L3 480MB shared)
experiment: synthetic random binary codes (1024 bits=128 B/vec), scan-only (knn_binary_funnel_tiled c=0). N=100k..100M x tile 1/8/16/32. q=cores*tile so every config runs exactly 8 base-passes on all 8 cores.
metrics: qps; gbps=cores*base_bytes/secs (real DRAM traffic, tile-independent); gcmp_per_s=q*N/secs (vector-comparisons/s, the compute rate)
note: NO L3->DRAM CLIFF for the binary scan. Gcmp/s is flat within ~15-20% across 100k->100M (1000x): ~0.6 at tile=1 (load-bound), ~1.1 at tile>=8 (popcount-compute-bound). The 128 B/vec codes are compact enough that DRAM BW (~80 GB/s aggregate) keeps the popcount units fed even at 12.8 GB. L3-resident (100k) gives ~98 GB/s vs DRAM ~78 GB/s = a gentle ~20% step, not a collapse. Tiling wins at EVERY scale: tile=1->8 ~1.8x (converts load-bound to compute-bound), then saturates at tile=8 (matches 038). It does NOT grow with N because the scan never becomes more memory-bound. This is exactly why binary scales and f32 does not: an f32 store (4 KB/vec, 32x bytes) would be hard DRAM-bound and cliff; binary stays compute-bound. Carousel never-cliffs survives at scale because the underlying shared scan does not cliff. 100M single-box: 6.2 QPS (tile=1) / 11 QPS (tile=8), 8 cores.
